The ancient Egyptians referred to their land as Kemet, which translates to "Black Land". This name was given to the region due to the rich, dark soil that was deposited by the Nile River, which made the land suitable for farming and fertile. The annual flooding of the Nile brought nutrient-rich silt that made the land highly productive, allowing the ancient Egyptians to grow crops and sustain a large population. In contrast, the surrounding desert areas were known as Deshret, or "Red Land", due to their arid and barren nature.
